This section overviews the system design and the proposed
aerial vehicle’s operational flow. The aerial platform adopts
the form of an H-shaped coaxial octocopter with the body
B
frame F = {OB; xB, yB, zB} attached to its CoG, as shown
in fig. 1. Concerning the simplified schematic of the system
model  in fig. 4,  the front rotors (1, 2, 6, and 7) have fixed
rotating axes parallel to zB while the back rotors (3, 4, 5, and
8) have tiltable axes that can pivot around yB simultaneously.
α ∈ [−90°,  90°] denotes  the  tilting angle between each
tiltable axis and zB. This design introduces an additional DoF
compared to classic aerial vehicles with 4-DoF actuation. The
distance from the system’s CoG to each rotor center along xB
is represented by L, while the distance along yB is denoted
as W.
Above all,  the platform also features a displaceable CoM
along the body axis xB, defined as the interaction axis. This
feature is enabled via sliding a plate equipped with heavy
components along a guiding frame parallel to xB, which we
call  the shifting-mass plate,  see fig. 1. The  length of  the
guiding frame is denoted as L0, see fig. 4. An EE is mounted at
the tip of the guiding frame to contact the work surface. During
a pushing task, firstly, the designed platform establishes stable
contact between the EE and the work surface, as in fig. 3c.
Once stable contact  is ensured,  the shifting-mass plate  is
moved toward the work surface along xB to enable higher
force exertion, as in fig. 3d.
We use the CoG of the shifting-mass plate to present  its
location. We denote lS as the length of the shifting-mass plate
along the body axis xB, as in fig. 4. The shifting-mass plate
position along xB w.r.t. the body frame origin OB is denoted
as l(t) ∈ [0, L + L0 − 0.5lS]. By changing the shifting-mass
plate position, the resultant displacement of the system’s CoM
along xB is denoted by d(t). When the system’s CoM is
located at d > L outside the rotor-defined area (i.e., over-

displaced),  the aerial vehicle often flips around the contact
area,  leading to instability. Therefore, d(t)  is restricted by
[0, L]  to avoid risky scenarios and damage to the platform.
Assuming the symmetric mass distribution of  the platform
around its body axes,  the relation between l(t) and d(t) is
given by:

A physical prototype as  in fig. 1, whose main physical
properties are shown in table I, is built from scratch using 3D
printed parts, carbon fiber tubes, relying on the system design
in section II.  Its mechatronic devices are listed in table II.
In the following, we detail the hardware of the built physical
prototype.
A. Propulsion System
The propulsion system includes the tiltable back rotors and
the fixed front  rotors. To tilt  the rotating axes of  the back
rotors simultaneously around the body axis yB, the rotor pairs
4&5 and 3&8 are rigidly attached to a carbon fiber tube. This
tube can be rotated via a pair of gears and a Dynamixel AX-
12A servo motor as shown in the bottom left figure of fig. 1.
This smart motor supports a total range of [0°, 300°] suitable
for the allowed tilting angle range. Each rotor is composed
of a FLYCOLOR Raptor5 Brushless ESC (Electronic Speed
Controller)  (3-6S,  45A),  a Turnigy D2826 motor,  and an
Aeronaut CAM Propeller. We define kt and kb as each rotor’s

JOURNAL OF LAT X CLASS FILES, VOL. 14, NO. 8, AUGUST 2021
E
thrust and drag torque coefficients. An RC-Benchmark Series
1580 Test Stand was used to identify the coefficients of the
−5
designed propulsion system, with kt = 1.156 · 10  and
kb = 0.0277 · kt. The system achieves the total thrust required
for gravity compensation with a reasonable speed ratio of
≈ 0.5 w.r.t. the maximum speed.
B. CoM Displacing
As mentioned in section II, the system’s CoM displacing is
achieved by changing the shifting-mass plate position along
the guiding frame. This sliding motion of the shifting-mass
plate is enabled by a linear actuator composed of a Dynamixel
XL430 servo mounted at  the guiding frame tip, a M6 lead
screw, and a M6 screw nut inset in a box rigidly attached to
the shifting-mass plate, illustrated in the bottom right figure of
fig. 1. This design ensures accurate, smooth, and slow motion
of the dynamical CoM displacing process. The selected servo
motor supports the extended position (multi-turn) mode, which
allows continuous mapping between the servo position and the
shifting-mass plate position l.
C. System Integration
A compliant EE is mounted at the tip of the guiding frame to
reduce the impact during initial contact with the work surface,
as in fig. 1. A high static friction coefficient material is used
at the EE tip to provide sufficient friction for stable pushing.
The Cubepilot Cube Orange Plus  is selected as  the flight
controller with the Lattepanda 3 Delta as the onboard compan-
ion computer. The hardware connectivity diagram, detailing
power distribution and communication interfaces is illustrated
in fig. 5. The Sky-Drones SmartAP PDB (Power Distribution
Board) distributes  the power  from the 4C LiPo battery to
the aerial vehicle’s different components. The flight controller
outputs PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) signals to command
the ESC to control  the motor power output  and rotating
speed. The  telemetry radio facilitates  remote configuration
and monitoring through QGroundControl, while the RC
(Radio-Controller) system handles manual control and safety
failsafe functionalities.

the main reference frames are depicted in fig. 4 illustrating

A. Moment of Inertia Estimation
Differently to conventional aerial vehicles, the shifting-mass
mechanism in fig. 4 affects the system’s inertia by varying the
1
shifting-mass plate position l(t) along the body axis xB . This
displacement leads to modifications in the moments of inertia
along the body axes yB and zB. Starting from the realistic
CAD models representing the platform in fig. 1, we employ
linear regression technology to obtain mathematical models of
Iyy and Izz w.r.t. l similarly to [24]:

V. CONTROL FRAMEWORK
Considering the modeled system’s under-actuation as intro-
duced in section IV and inspired by [5], [13], [25], we employ
a cascade control pipeline as summarized in fig. 6 with the ca-
pability to handle both free flights and physical interactions. In
∗
the following, (·)  indicate the desired quantities. This control
framework integrates low-level geometric attitude control and
high-level selective impedance control outputting the desired
∗  ∗  R3
actuation wrenches Fa , Γa ∈  . These outputs are sent to
the control allocation to compute the desired rotors’ rotating
∗  ∗
speed Ωi and tilting angle α .

This section describes the software integration to implement
the designed control  framework on the developed hardware
prototype in section III. The proposed architecture in fig. 7 is
intended to establish a foundation for the future development
of  applications built  on  this platform creating a  reusable
framework that can be adapted for developing future custom
aerial manipulation platforms.
The integration focuses on several key quality attributes
3
according to the ISO/IEC 25010  standard:
• Safety and Reliability: Ensuring safety with fail-safes,
pre-flight checks, and reliable software to minimize risks.
• Real-time Performance: Achieving stable flight through
consistent timing and bounded scheduling latency.
• Control Performance: Supporting precise motion con-
trol with low steady-state errors, emphasizing stability
over rapid trajectory tracking.
• Extensibility: Designing a modular architecture compat-
ible with future custom platforms and tasks, using ROS2
for flexibility.
• Maintainability: Facilitating easy updates and debugging
through a container-based development environment and
diagnostic tools.
• Usability:  Integrating with user-friendly interfaces like
QGroundControl for effective prototyping.
A. Detailed Software Architecture
The developed software runs on the companion computer
in ad-hoc Docker image using ROS2 and communicates with
the flight controller via µXRCE-DDS protocol where PX4
[26] runs, as in fig. 7. A key feature of the PX4 Autopilot
is the flight mode which triggers different UAV behaviors.
The host computer is only for flight-mode management and
monitoring using QGroundControl which communicates
with the flight controller through the MAVLink protocol. The
ROS2 node - Aerobull Control Node, contains the customized
control framework introduced in section V and the shifting-
mass control which maps the servo position into the shifting-
mass plate position.
Being different from the standards PX4 flight modes,  this
architecture includes a Custom Mode based on the PX4 ROS2
4
Interface Library , a new ROS2-based C++ library expanding
and simplifying the external  interfacing capabilities of  the
3
https://iso25000.com/index.php/en/iso-25000-standards/iso-25010
4
https://docs.px4.io/main/en/ros2/px4 ros2 interface lib.html

6
PX4 Autopilot. This library provides a high-level Control
Interface to facilitate  the creation of customized con-
trol  logic and a Navigation Interface for  external
localization sources  integration. We set  the Navigation
Interface interconnecting the Optitrack system to the EKF
(Extended Kalman Filter) state estimation module within PX4
using the relative ROS2 driver  running in the companion
computer.
B. Control Interface
The Control Interface enables the dynamic registra-
tion of flight modes in ROS2 mapped to the concept of robot
behaviors, such as taking off, landing, or navigation. The Off-
4
board Mode  as one of the standard PX4 flight modes allows
sending setpoints to PX4 while offering key advantages:  it
supports multiple on-demand applications triggered by events
allowing custom modes with specific requirements and safety
checks integrated into PX4’s arming and failsafe logic ensuring
safe operations. The Aerobull Custom Mode is defined starting
from the Offboard Mode enabling autonomous navigation and
physical interaction using the customized controller in fig. 6
by sending setpoints.
To do so, we extend the current PX4 ROS2 Control
Interface capabilities to support the customized controller
abstraction. The developed Control Interface with di-
∗
rect actuator setpoints u  as in fig. 7 bypass the default PX4
cascaded control stack to be implemented in the ROS2 domain.
This implementation allows abstaining completely from the
PX4 architecture, serving as an alternative approach to ad-hoc
firmware modifications [27],  [28]. Switching to the original
flight stack using the RC is always possible preserving the
original safety layers and controllers.
Considering that the shifting-mass dynamics are slower than
the tilting angle dynamics and that  the system control  law
varies with the shifting-mass plate position,  the companion
computer handles the whole-body control running each loop
and sending commands at  the same frequency as the native
flight  controller. The  running frequencies are depicted  in
fig. 7. Moreover, we leverage the Linux real-time kernel or
PREEMPT_RT patch to improve the reliability of the controller
execution. Our goal is to ensure the operating system runs the
ROS2 control  loop timer every 4ms (250Hz) with minimal
scheduling latency,  thereby preventing delays in the actuator
commands sent to PX4.

This experimental section tested two case studies of physical
interaction tasks with and without displacing the system CoM
using the developed platform in fig. 1 for benchmarking. With
the experiments, we aim to emphasize the proposed platform
characteristics and efficient high-force generation capability.
In our previous work [23], we achieved a pushing force of
15N with the developed platform. In this article, we present
the additional experiments during which we achieved 28N
of pushing force which is almost  the same as  the system
gravity force (30.5N). The corresponding experimental video
is available here: https://youtu.be/vMauKy6UpIU.

A. Experiment Setup
The experiments were carried out in an indoor environment
equipped with an OptiTrack system. An aluminum crane with
a safety rope was used to avoid platform damage in case of
a crash, and a vertical wooden board was mounted on the
crane as the work surface (see fig. 3b). The OptiTrack system
tracks the pose of the work surface and the UAV. The table III
provides  the tuned controller gains while the aerodynamic
coefficients of rotors are displayed in section II. In both cases,
the platform was commanded to approach and push on the
work surface with a series of position setpoints culminating
with the ones “behind” the work surface. We denote δp as
the distance from the work surface to the setpoint along the
interaction axis xB, and δp > 0 for the ones “behind” the
surface. In practice, the maximum shifting-mass plate position
m
was configured such that lmax = 0.18m ≪ m L = 0.48m

In Case 1, δp = 0.6m was sent to the platform to establish
an initial contact with the work surface. While preserving
the stable contact with  the work surface and  the system
orientation,  the shifting-mass plate was shifted toward the
work surface until its maximum position 0.18m, as introduced
in [23], where the system tracking capabilities are already
demonstrated. With the displaced system CoM resulted by
moving the shifting-mass plate, position setpoints with δp =
0.8m,  1.0m,  1.2m were sent  to the platform to exert ever-
increasing pushing forces on the work surface.
In Case 2,  the platform was commanded to push on the
work surface with δp = 0.4m, 0.6m, 0.8m, 1.0m while the
shifting-mass plate remained still  (i.e., fixed system CoM).
In the following, we present the experimental results of both
cases.
B. Case 1
Concerning the previously achieved 15N of pushing force
in [23], in this case, the platform pushed on the work surface
starting with a setpoint  0.6m “behind”  the work surface.
Without changing the setpoint location, the shifting-mass plate
was sent forward along the body axis xB while the system
consistently exerted a pushing force of around 13N, see fig. 8a.
Different  setpoints were sent  to  the high-level  impedance
controller after achieving the maximum shifting-mass plate
position at  l = 0.18m. With δp = 0.8m,  the platform

With this paper, we extended the results achieved in [23]
where a novel  aerial manipulation platform is presented.
This platform features a displaceable CoM and tiltable back
rotors, tailored to advanced aerial manipulation tasks on non-
horizontal surfaces. In this paper, we focused on the system
design, hardware and software integration, and the platform’s

8
performance during the task execution in terms of force gen-
eration. The benefit of CoM displacement for efficient high-
force interaction is highlighted in the experiments compared
to the platform with a fixed CoM configuration. We identified
promising future revenue by presenting a novel solution to
enhance high-force tool manipulation with aerial vehicles in
industrial applications.
